name: HyPer and Umbra — Produce/Consume SQL Compilation
summary: Thomas Neumann's HyPer (SIGMOD 2011) introduced produce/consume codegen: compile a SQL query plan into a single fused tight loop. 10× over iterator-model interpreters. Umbra (CIDR 2020) replaced LLVM with a custom backend to cut compile time at the cost of 10–30% worse code quality.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Thomas Neumann's **HyPer** (SIGMOD 2011, "Efficiently Compiling Efficient Query Plans for Modern Hardware") introduced the produce/consume model for SQL compilation. A query plan is a tree of operators (scan, filter, join, aggregate); instead of each operator iterating over tuples from below (the classical iterator model, "volcano style"), the compiler generates a single fused loop where each operator's `produce` method generates code that calls the parent's `consume` method.

The result is a query compiled to one tight loop over the input rows with no virtual dispatch, no per-tuple function calls, and no materialisation of intermediate results. Performance gains are dramatic — 10× or more over iterator-model interpreters on analytical queries.

sections.title: Umbra — successor with custom backend
sections.content:
**Umbra** (Neumann et al., CIDR 2020) is HyPer's successor, incorporating lessons from a decade of production use. Key evolution: Umbra uses a custom low-level IR and a fast backend instead of LLVM, because LLVM compilation time became the bottleneck for short analytical queries. The trade-off is 10–30% slower generated code for 5–10× faster compilation — the right choice when the query runs once and the plan is large.

The broader lesson: **for short-running queries, JIT compile time competes directly with execution time**. Umbra's custom backend is the analytical-database equivalent of Cranelift's compile-speed-over-quality choice for Wasm.
